Q1: I tried to keep things pretty structured, honestly. I'd set aside time most days to work through course materials, usually around 25 hours a week spread across the week. I'm a visual learner so I'd watch the videos, make diagrams, and organize my notes in a way that made sense to me. The routine helped me stay on track even when I wasn't feeling super motivated about the content itself.
Q2: I definitely aimed for regular times, but I'll admit it varied more than I'd like. I had my extracurriculars and other commitments that sometimes threw things off. When deadlines approached, I'd shift gears and focus more intensely, but generally I tried to maintain a steady pace throughout the week rather than cramming everything at once.
Q3: Usually it was just habit, like checking in to see if anything new was posted or if there were announcements. Sometimes it was because I knew I had to watch certain videos or check on assignment details. I logged in pretty frequently, maybe just out of routine more than anything else. It became almost automatic after a while.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks it just became something I did without thinking much about it. I'd wake up, check my phone, then eventually open the LMS while eating breakfast or between activities. The peer support system we had going helped too because my study group would remind each other to check things. It wasn't exciting, but it was consistent.
Q5: The videos were definitely the most useful for me since I learn best visually. I watched a lot of them, probably over 160 minutes worth, and I'd pause and take notes or rewind parts I didn't get. The discussion boards were okay too because seeing how other students understood concepts sometimes clicked things into place for me better than just reading alone.
Q6: Some of the reading materials I'd skim or skip entirely if I felt the videos covered the same stuff. There were certain discussion threads that felt repetitive or not that relevant, so I'd just scroll past those. If something didn't seem immediately connected to assignments or exams, I'd deprioritize it. I know that's not ideal but time is limited.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to what I'd be tested on or what I needed for assignments, it felt worth it. Also if it was presented in a visual or engaging way, I was more likely to pay attention. Activities that felt like busywork or just checking boxes didn't really capture my interest. I needed to see the point of it.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different, like they were extras I could skip if I was busy or bored. Required ones I'd do regardless of how I felt because they affected my grade. With optional stuff, I'd only engage if I was genuinely curious or had extra time, which wasn't often given everything else going on.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download slides and PDFs and study them on my tablet without being connected. I also used external YouTube videos sometimes when the course explanations weren't clicking for me. My textbook got a fair amount of use too, especially before exams. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or working through practice problems on paper where the LMS wouldn't register anything. I'd also discuss concepts with my study group in person or over chat apps. Those conversations were really helpful but obviously don't show up in any log.
Q11: Yeah, I did this all the time actually. I'd download a video or PDF once, then work through it multiple times offline. The LMS would only show that single access, but I might have spent hours with that material over several days. It was more convenient than streaming everything repeatedly.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where I felt pretty bored with the content, honestly. The material just wasn't grabbing me even though I knew I needed to get through it. When my stress levels were high from other classes or activities, it got harder to make myself care about logging in and engaging meaningfully.
Q13: For sure. When I had a lot going on with my extracurriculars or other coursework piling up, the LMS became more of a quick check-in than deep engagement. Fatigue was real too, especially later in the semester. I'd log in but not really absorb much because my brain was just tired.
Q14: Plenty of times. I'd have videos playing but find myself zoning out or thinking about other things. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them carefully. I was technically active but not really present mentally. It happens when you're going through the motions.
Q15: I definitely put off some assignments even when I knew they mattered. Usually it was because they seemed tedious or I wasn't in the right headspace. I'd tell myself I'd do it later when I felt more focused, but sometimes that meant rushing at the last minute. It's not my proudest habit.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main motivator for LMS activity spikes. I'd ramp up engagement significantly when something was due soon. Without a deadline looming, I'd maintain my routine but at a lower intensity. The structure of due dates kept me accountable even when I was feeling bored with the material.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially on assignments where I scored well, it motivated me to keep up my approach. If feedback was critical, I'd try to adjust but sometimes I'd feel a bit discouraged first. The peer support helped here because we'd discuss feedback together and figure out how to improve.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to follow, which I appreciated since I like having a routine. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me plan my week. But sometimes the structure felt rigid and didn't account for when life got busy or when I needed more flexibility.
Q19: The video content being available to download made things easier for my learning style. What made it harder was when there were too many small tasks that felt disconnected from the bigger picture. Also, my attendance being low meant I sometimes missed context that would have helped me engage better with online materials.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for things, trying to figure out what they need, or just browsing without a clear plan. They could be stressed and clicking around without actually producing anything. Or maybe they're preparing and researching but haven't gotten to the submission stage yet.
Q21: That actually sounds a bit like me in some ways. My exam scores and assignment scores were decent even though I wasn't always super active in the LMS. I studied a lot offline with downloaded materials and my study group. The LMS activity doesn't capture all the real learning that happens elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in, what you click on, and how long you're technically on a page. But they miss whether you're actually focused, whether you understood anything, or whether you learned more effectively somewhere else. They're measuring presence, not comprehension or real engagement.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually thinking about the material and trying to understand it, not just going through motions. It's when I'm curious about something or working to connect new ideas to what I already know. It's not about how many times I logged in but whether the content actually stuck with me.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my study group sessions where we'd work through problems together and explain concepts to each other. It wouldn't show the times I was genuinely thinking about course material while walking to class or doing other things. And it definitely wouldn't capture that even when I was bored, I was still committed to passing and learning what I needed to.
